ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (beef meat, grilled) is the closest defensible exact-category match available. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Malay-style marinated beef satay with peanut sauce; the satay marinade and peanut sauce components are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef satay as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Satay Daging consists of approximately 5 beef skewers (around 250 g total), typically served with peanut dipping sauce, ketupat (compressed rice), fresh cucumber slices, and raw onion wedges.

nutrient_source.title: Beef satay, grilled — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore Foodo composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia MyFCD; HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Satay Daging are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD grilled beef entries and HPB Singapore Foodo satay records. Input values (ai_generated, enriched_json, confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these approved sources. Core macros, sodium, and micronutrients have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef, ground, 80% lean, broiled (FDC ID 174036) as a secondary anchor. Values retained from input where consistent with approved-source ranges; saturated fat estimated from USDA beef grilled data. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g set to -1 where detailed fatty acid and moisture data for this specific Malay preparation are not individually reported in the primary approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for marinated grilled beef with peanut sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Satay Daging exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) grilled beef has negligible GI contribution; (2) peanut sauce has a low GI (~14 per University of Sydney GI Database for peanuts); (3) palm sugar marinade contributes moderate GI (~54); (4) the composite dish is dominated by protein and fat, which attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (40 × 16.25) / 100 = 6.5. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 6.5 × 2.5 = 16.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (350 mg per 100 g; ~875 mg per 250 g serving) from marinade and peanut sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce servings, as a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 875 mg sodium, representing 38% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (6.5 g per 100 g), attenuated by high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Satay Daging has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of 6.5 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large portions of ketupat (compressed rice).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~4.8 g per 100 g) from beef and peanut sauce, and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake due to saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) and sodium (350 mg per 100 g); choosing leaner beef cuts and limiting peanut sauce quantity can reduce cardiovascular risk while preserving the protein benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, galangal) in marinade; potential pro-inflammatory effect of charcoal-grilled red meat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric and galangal in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory compounds; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to charcoal-grilled red meat should monitor personal tolerance and consider limiting frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Satay Daging (Beef Satay) is a classic Malay grilled meat dish consisting of marinated beef pieces threaded onto bamboo skewers and cooked over a charcoal fire. The marinade is a fragrant blend of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander seeds, cumin, and palm sugar, which tenderises the beef and imparts a complex savory-sweet-smoky flavour profile. The charcoal grilling creates a lightly charred exterior while keeping the interior juicy. It is traditionally served with kuah kacang (spicy peanut dipping sauce), ketupat (compressed rice cakes), fresh cucumber slices, and raw onion wedges. Nutritionally, Satay Daging is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish providing approximately 16.5 g protein and 13.5 g fat per 100 g, with a relatively low carbohydrate content of 6.5 g per 100 g. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), making it a suitable option for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate at 350 mg per 100 g, primarily from the marinade and peanut sauce. It is a good source of zinc (4.5 mg), iron (2.2 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), and niacin (4.5 mg). Satay Daging is widely consumed across Malaysia and Singapore as street food, festival fare, and a main course at family gatherings. It is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef satay?
Is beef satay suitable for diabetics?
What is the glycemic index of satay?
How much protein in beef satay?
What sauce is served with satay daging?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo approved-source ranges for grilled beef satay, and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy grilled beef data. Input provenance is ai_generated (confidence 0.9), which is treated as medium confidence under Veritas policy. Saturated fat was estimated from USDA grilled beef data. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and water content are not individually reported for this specific preparation in primary approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for Malay-style marinated charcoal-grilled beef satay are not individually reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD, HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of April 2026. Saturated fat (4.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy grilled ground beef 80% lean (FDC ID 174036) as the closest approved-source analogue. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database peanut and palm sugar GI values combined with the known protein-fat attenuation effect on glycemic response; no direct GI measurement for Satay Daging exists in the approved GI database. Nutrition values represent the composite dish including marinade and peanut sauce as typically served; values will vary if ketupat is included in the weighed portion. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.0), sodium_mg (350.0), saturated_fat_g (4.8), sugar_g (4.2) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and consistent with approved-source ranges for grilled beef satay.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (40), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (6.5), and gi_source all present. GI is an analogous estimate grounded in University of Sydney GI Database peanut and palm sugar values with documented estimation basis.

SNOMED CT set to null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is over 1,100 characters, well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
